dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: On this episode, we discuss Zendaya and her sensational performance in the black and white two person drama, Malcolm and Marie. We discuss unreliable narrators, aggressive food eating acting, and the dangers of filmmakers tussling with critics. If you're enjoying the show, we need your help. Take two minutes and leave us a five star rating and a review wherever you listen to your podcast. It really does help new listeners find the show. And now without further a-Dune, Malcolm and Marie. [theme music fades out]

dialogues.text: Alright. Here we go. Malcom and Marie is the quest for artistic achievement, and the imperative to exercise the demons that haunt you. Malcolm is a talented young filmmaker at the end of the biggest night of his life, the successful premiere of his first feature film. Marie, his girlfriend, an addict and former actress is hurt and upset at Malcolm's lack of appreciation for her. Holed up in a stunning house on a cliff, they will spend all night locked in a fierce argument. Digging Deeper and deeper until they uncover their own painful truths. Will they be transformed and emerge together stronger than ever? Or is this the end of Malcolm and Marie?

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: So the way this film, Malcolm and Marie came together was that Sam Levinson is the writer and director, producer, showrunner of Euphoria. And they were greenlit to start shooting season two, on a Friday of Euphoria. And on Monday, all of Hollywood got shut down. This is back in March of last year. And so he and Zendaya talked on the phone every day. And after a while, they started thinking, well, what can we do? And basically, they pitched the idea of she's like, 'Why don't we just shoot at my house? We'll do a small cast and crew, we'll make it happen.' And they ended up putting together this film, with a super small cast, they quarantined together, there were only 12 people allowed on set at a time. So it was pretty incredible what they were able to pull together on the show.

dialogues.text: So quickly, just from the cinematography perspective, this was shot by Marcell Rév, who is the cinematographer for Euphoria, and was also the cinematographer for Assassination Nation. Which is the other movie directed by Sam Levenson.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Yeah. Have you seen it?

dialogues.name: HAITCH I
dialogues.text: have not.

dialogues.name: JASON I
dialogues.text: have not. But that's the movie incidentally, like Sam Levinson's prior film that got a like bad review from LA Times critic Katie Walsh. [fact bell rings]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Really? Ohhh my God.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yeah. So that's like, that's like the sort of that's, that's like the sort of the kind of uses, he kind of uses the Malcolm character to take shots at a specific critic.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Oh my God! That's hilarious.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Which didn't, which did not escaped the attention of -

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Said critic.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yeah, like or anyone like, you know.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Oh now I want to read that.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: That was really funny.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: You could definitely feel that like, it was like certain things that Malcolm was saying. I was like, this feels like a little pointed and a little personal. Like, I think, right. You could tell where it was like, Sam, just specifically venting about his own experiences. And then when it was like more so the character but, yeah. [Boom laughs]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Right. Well, Malcolm is needy, right. Like, that's the deal.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Quite, yes. I'd say so.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: So as you know, he's doing this the pacing around and thinking about it, we begin to see very clearly that Marie is pissed. And she's making him mac and cheese. And he's saying, "What are you angry about?" as the jazz music swells, and it's like, really intense. And then you just have the shot of the mac and cheese and boom, credits. And I love, I love that. And then basically, she says - [clip of Malcolm and Marie plays]

dialogues.name: MARIE
dialogues.text: It's not just about you forgetting to thank me, Malcolm. It's about how you see me, and how you view my contribution, not just to this relationship, but to your work, specifically in a movie you made about my life. [clip of Malcolm and Marie ends]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: And that's sort of the central beginning point was that he hasn't he didn't thank her at the award show. And I love how you know, we'll unfold it as we get through the film here, but I love the way that just starts like he fucked up, didn't say thank you tonight at the award show.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: That is a fuckup though. He did fuck it up. He's gonna get, he's gonna get like the angry mac and cheese for that for sure.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Hundred percent. The tension like in the prologue was like building so much and I was just like, okay, what she mad about though? What she mad about? And then when it finally comes out I was like, damn! Malcolm! How dare you? [Boom & Haitch laugh]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: So he denies that it's about her life. And so she presses him about it and I love the scene where he's sitting there eating the mac and cheese, you know, aggressively with his fork and his tie thrown over his shoulder.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Oh my gosh. [clip of Malcolm and Marie plays]

dialogues.name: MALCOLM
dialogues.text: Imani is not based on you!

dialogues.name: MARIE
dialogues.text: Oh so she's a 20 year old drug addict trying to get clean? Was that just a pure fucking coincidence?

dialogues.name: MALCOLM
dialogues.text: Obviously, you getting cleaned was a part of the inspiration!

dialogues.name: MARIE
dialogues.text: At 20?

dialogues.name: MALCOLM
dialogues.text: Yes. And you were able to provide genuine insight into that, what that was like, but Imani's not fucking based on you! It's an amalgamation of all different things! A whole bunch of things!

dialogues.name: MARIE
dialogues.text: Who?

dialogues.name: MALCOLM
dialogues.text: People!

dialogues.name: MARIE
dialogues.text: What people?!

dialogues.name: MALCOLM A
dialogues.text: lot of different people. [clip of Malcolm and Marie ends]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: People! That was a great line read.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Well, I was just gonna say him eating the mac and cheese was like a full body cringe moment for me. It was so aggressive. But it was almost, because Haitch, I also did a second pass. Like I watched it the first time. And it was kind of hard for me to just see past how much I hated Malcolm. And then the second time, it was a lot better. And I kind of saw like a little bit more of the comedy in it. And just like the way he was acting like an absolute child, like when, exactly what you said like when he's just gives that like look, and he's like 'people'. And there's also a moment beforehand where she storms off and he looks at the mac and cheese and then he looks and he's like, 'should I go after her?' And then he just goes and eats the mac and cheese. [Boom laughs] It's funny.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: That's really funny.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: They filmed it sequentially. Like they filmed like the movie in sequence.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Really?

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yeah. And like and so I wonder about like, sort of like what he had to go do like, you know directly after just housing this bowl of mac and cheese. [Boom laughs] 'Cause like you know, because he really fucking like tears into it. Like you can kind of tell sometimes when people are fake eating in movies. And he is mowing down on some Kraft.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: He's giving it.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: There's some aggressive foley work with the you know, like fork, teeth smashing.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: The mac and cheese sound? It made me want mac and cheese. I'll say that. [Haitch laughs]

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: It was reminded me of like, the way people, like you've been out all night like partying maybe, and then you come home and just crush a burrito, crush a bowl of mac and cheese. I was like that's the vibe, right? [Boom laughs]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Totally. So they do have, there's a little more argument. She says 'you don't you don't care about somebody until you're about to lose them, that you start to pay attention.' And he does say - this I thought, I found interesting. He said that he had a draft before he met her. So I thought that was kind of interesting, because that does shoot a hole in her theory a little bit. But he does agree. She says 'I want you to admit the movies better for me being with you.' And he agrees and they make up sort of step one.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Right, first making up.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: First round done.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: You knew it wasn't gonna hold. You knew for sure that wasn't gonna...

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH

dialogues.name: MARIE
dialogues.text: Next thing you know, you will be on your press tour for your new Lego film. Talk about how it's an allegory for failures of reconstruction. Well, I mean, the original working title was 40 Legos and a Mule. But the studio got all uhhh.. [clip of Malcolm and Marie ends]

dialogues.name: BOOM

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: 40 Legos and a Mule, yeah, that was a good one. That's a good one.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Yeah, no, it was interesting, because it was like funny. And it was nice to see them kind of like or was, like inside of like when they're good, they're good, kind of thing. And then kind of set up for what she says later on. About like the hoe-ing in the industry. [Boom laughs]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Yeah, exactly. We'll get there. We will get there.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Yeah, we'll get there. [Boom laughs]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: So she goes outside to have a cigarette and I Forgot To Be Your Lover by William Bell plays. [clip of Malcolm and Marie plays, I Forgot To Be Your Lover by William Bell is playing]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH

dialogues.name: JASON

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: As well as Euphoria, which has unbelievable music. Nina Simone - [clip of Do What You Gotta Do by Nina Simone plays]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Duke Ellington, War, Sam Cooke, Otis Redding - [clip of These Arms Of Mine by Otis Redding plays]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Donny Hathaway. And then like some modern really cool music, Michael Kiwanuka - [clip of The Final Frame by Michael Kiwanuka plays]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Frankie Reyes - [clip of Flor de Azalea by Frankie Reyes plays]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: It was just the music through this entire film is so top notch.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: It is really great. Yeah. And just nice touches to very specific moments. I think it really works. And when he plays that song, it's funny. But it's also kind of like you could see what, because he's a creative. He expresses himself in various ways, right? So of course, he's just, he just doesn't want to fight anymore. So he puts this song on, and he thinks it's gonna be like this nice touching moment, but it just gets her even more riled up.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Yeah, yeah, she calls him on it, right?

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Yeah.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: She's like a song written for another woman is not going to help.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Another woman. Yeah!

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: So then we have the scene where he makes the case, actually, that he did support her. And he said that, you know, he was the one who checked her in rehab. He was the one who went to group therapy. He was the one who when she left and went to a meeting, but instead went to score drugs and have sex with another guy, that he was there for her.

dialogues.name: JASON I
dialogues.text: think he lays it on. He, I don't he like consistently sort of escalates to like, intimacy in this movie. And like, it's a really bad look for like a boyfriend. You're just like oh, it's not gonna work out well, if your go-to move is like, you know, let me tell you about some other women and like -

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Oh, God. It was rough. Because any time, okay, especially in my first watch, I just felt very like, like, my guard was up. I was like, I do not like this Malcolm fellow. Because when he just kind of right off the jump, I feel like Marie's, you know, I'm like she's worked up and she's saying things. She's saying hurtful things. But when he's saying things, it's like, he's really like cutting, right? When he's, like when he's like scarfing down the mac and cheese, and he calls her unstable. And even when he's saying those things about like, having supported her, it's like, starts with a very, like, deep and terrible insult. And then he goes into that kind of talk. And I'm like, I'm really having a hard time sympathizing with you here. They both get quite ugly. But like Jason said, it's, it's a bad look for a boyfriend. So it's hard to kind of like, mentally just be able to sympathize with him.

dialogues.name: HAITCH I
dialogues.text: had some of that same vibe for sure. What I, what I found interesting was trying to understand who is, you know, to the extent they can be, who is right about what's happening here?

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Yeah.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: And, and I think interestingly, when he says that he was there for her, and that he loved her, and that's what, you know, he was he was there in those darkest moments, and he was willing to sacrifice. And she just says, 'you're only doing it for the material.' Like for her. That's true. That's what, that's what she's afraid of. But it's not clear that that's true.

dialogues.name: JASON I
dialogues.text: think he's a bit of a vortex. I think he like is like sort of his needs, like kind of come first at this point in the relationship. Like we don't see the time in which like, he's like there for her during like, her struggles with addiction or whatever. But at this time, it like feels like his needs and like his, you know, sort of emotional needs, his career needs, everything else, I've kind of become like the vortex of their relationship. And so I like for me, like sort of his shittiness as a boyfriend, like kind of make me call into question like his artistic critique as well. Like, it sort of like, not that, I think he's got like, I think it's well said or whatever. It's like an interesting argument. But, like, it makes me, it makes me like, kind of, it makes me like sort of doubt him or treat him as like an unreliable narrator or whatever. Like where I'm just like, I don't know about this guy. And like, the movie like not to, like, you know, jump to the end, kind of follows his path where you kind of know that she's going to have the last word on this one. That like, you know, that like, he shoots his shot too early. He kind of puts it all out there his case about why he's right. And you're like, well, that's not gonna work. It's only end of act one. And you kind of know that like she's gonna end up, she's gonna end up with the upper hand and in this argument.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Or at least you're like hoping, like because by the time you get to the third act like there, it's I'm just like, I don't know, you know, like is she? But you're just hoping she does.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Well, but so I mean, I definitely started in the exact same vibe that you guys were in the sense of saying like, I'm going to side with Marie and like, I'll hear him out. And that scene in the bathtub where he goes hard, he lays out all the exes that he's been with. And he's calling out how he got different things of the character Imani from the movie, not from her but from other people. But - [clip of Malcolm and Marie plays]

dialogues.name: MALCOLM
dialogues.text: You wanna know the part of Imani that's based on you? It's the end. The part that makes it such a tragedy. The part as she, where she loathes herself so much because of all the guilt and the shame. She can't let the good in. That's the part that's based on you. Her inability to fathom that there's someone on this planet earth that just loves her. [clip of Malcolm and Marie ends]

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: And that, to me is a really important thing. Like if, if she's not capable of believing that she's worthy of love, then she may be reading into his intentions. That's not to say that he is right for being hurtful when he is hurt. He should be in more control of himself. But I think that's the dynamic I was picking up on.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Yeah. In this fight, they're trying to kind of like uncover the truth from the other person. But they've already made up like what they think that is, like so for Malcolm he thinks that like Marie's like not supporting him and jealous of Taylor and all that and for Marie I think she thinks that he - I guess kind of the same thing. Like, you know, he doesn't respect her and he takes her for granted and they're just both like not budging from those standpoints. Like the entire time. Does that makes sense?

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: It does. I think, I think too, though, like, his, you know, like the idea of making this movie, which is so clearly inspired by his relationship with her and her life. And then like both not thanking her, like not really involving her in it any creative or emotional way like does seem pretty fucked up.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Yeah.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Yeah.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Fair enough. Fair enough. I think that, and there's a good discussion about that in just a minute but after the scene in the bathtub, which is amazing. I thought it's very interesting how the entire power dynamic shifts right? She comes out of the tub in her underwear and she just her makeup is off and she just looks really vulnerable. And it feels like the power has shifted to Malcolm and then he just completely falls apart when he gets the review for the LA Times white girl. [Boom laughs] And it's behind a paywall. $1.99 a month?!

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yeah, this is great.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: So Jason is this the backstory? Like the fact that this LA Times writer is wrong on the details, she doesn't know what the shots are called, the lenses.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yeah, I don't know if like if all of that is accurate to like her review of Assassination Nation like that she got technical details or whatever but like it's like largely believed to be like, you know, oh, like she gave, Katie Walsh of the LA Times gave his movie Assassination Nation a less than glowing review and then he's going to use this movie to like fuck it up. Now the additional kind of fucked up thing about it is that like Sam Levinson is a white guy and he puts this, he like kind of filters this attack on her through a racial lens. Which like, is not -

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Not cool, bro.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Super chill. [Boom & Jason laugh] Like he's he's kind of able, because the movie is -

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Is it screenwriting, is it screenwriting blackface? [Boom & Haitch laugh]

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yeah exactly! So I mean, I think that's a little fucked up. Like it's sort of to me kind of makes Sam Levinson and Malcolm seem more alike where it's like, oh, you are both like kind of emotionally needy people somehow or like, you know, you, Sam Levinson got this like bad review for your last movie and now you need to invent this like whole other movie just to like deal with that. [Boom laughs] Bringing all these other layers to like process it through. It feels like a lot.

dialogues.name: BOOM I
dialogues.text: don't know if specifically, like if this was like what came of the Katie Walsh review, but I know like the general kind of critique of Assassination Nation was that it's like a very kind of, it takes this kind of like feminist standpoint. But it is very evident that it's written by a man. I think like that's what the critics were saying about it. So, it's interesting to me that he then takes - and I've listened, I've listened to interviews, I think his like intention of casting black actors and even his reasoning for making the film black and white. Um, you know, I think the intentions are great in there. But it's always interesting when, especially because it's hard to separate. It's like, where, where Sam ends and where Malcolm kind of begins a little bit. So, it's an interesting choice.

dialogues.name: HAITCH I
dialogues.text: will say, I went to you know, I do my clips before we record, I try and grab some clips and think about what stuff really hit me. This scene is like, nine minutes long of one single long take rant by John David Washington. And it's fucking amazing. [clip of Malcolm and Marie plays]

dialogues.name: MALCOM
dialogues.text: [yelling] I mean, the fact that Barry Jenkins isn't gay, is that what made Moonlight so fucking universal?! Or is being gay the reason Coker empathize with women more than men. It's all a fucking mystery is the point. What drives a filmmaker?! What drives an artist?! Tell me that! Can you tell me that?! Fucking Karen! Can you tell me that?! No, you can't, can you? Because why? Because nobody knows! Who the fuck knows! Who the fuck knows! I mean, why did I shoot the scene - [clip of Malcolm and Marie ends]

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: It is really good.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Does the male gaze exist for gay directors?! It's a mystery Karennnn! [Boom laughs] Who the fuck knows?!

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: It's great. I mean, like, that's the thing about this movie is like its shortcomings notwithstanding, like, the performance is really sparkle. It looks awesome.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Incredible!

dialogues.name: JASON

dialogues.text: Think about how hard it is for Zendaya to be sitting there filming for nine minutes.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Just listening to him yelling.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Yeah. And she's not going crazy, right? She's just chilling.

dialogues.name: BOOM

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH

You know, you're playing dress up. Stop taking yourself and making yourself so important.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Love it.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: This is their second reconciliation. And it looks like they're trying to maybe get get to something going on. But he has to pee. [Boom laughs] So he leaves and just -

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Poor guy had to pee. That was a big mistake. If we hadn't had to pee, it all would have been alright.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: No, we wouldn't have gotten all the way through. But, the shot of her laying there. And you just know you can read it on our face. It's like the pain is right there. And then the single tear breaks down.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Oh, yeah.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: It's just like, holy cow!

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Go Zendaya. She took us there. Yeah, so her, her in that moment was just amazing. Because when he's just like, stay like this, like don't, like don't get mad and runs off.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Right, right. Think happy thoughts.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Right, right, right.

dialogues.name: BOOM

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Hmm!

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Love it. Love that.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Yeah. And she kills it. dialogues.text: Zendaya is great. That reminds me, a brief sidebar. dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Which she like strenuously denies. [clip of Zendaya on the Graham Norton Show plays]

dialogues.name: ZENDAYA

dialogues.name: ZENDAYA

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: And like wanted to use this opportunity on like, British television -

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: To clear the air. Yeah, so it's really good. It's really good story. And she's really animated telling it. Even in the Spider-Man movies, like she's so dour. Yeah, she's so like, like, about to cry about, you know, all the time. And so it's fun to see her, like just being like, she's a big goofball.

dialogues.name: BOOM
dialogues.text: Totally.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: So now we get to the crux of the of the issue. And the reason why she's, why she's crying, he comes back in and she says, 'Why didn't you cast me? Why didn't you fight for me?' Like she can't exercise her demon now or she feels like she can't.
